title: Human systems and resource use
summary: This lesson explores the concept of human carrying capacity and the tools used to measure our impact on Earth's systems. It focuses on the Ecological Footprint (EF) as a model for quantifying resource consumption and compares it with biocapacity to assess sustainability at local, national, and global scales.
learningObjectives: Explain the concept of carrying capacity for human populations and the challenges in its calculation.
Define and calculate the Ecological Footprint (EF) as a measure of resource demand.
Compare the EF with biocapacity to evaluate ecological deficit and credit.
Analyse the differences in EF between populations at different levels of economic development.

simpleExplanation.title: Earth's Budget: Are We Overspending?
simpleExplanation.summary: The Ecological Footprint measures our demand on nature, like our personal spending. Biocapacity is nature's budget, or its ability to regenerate resources. If our footprint exceeds biocapacity, we are in 'ecological overshoot', like going into debt.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine your bank account is Earth's biocapacity – the amount of resources the planet can regenerate each year. Your lifestyle – what you eat, how you travel, the energy you use – is your spending, which we call your Ecological Footprint. For a long time, humanity's spending was well within its means. Now, we are spending our 'income' for the year by August and living on credit for the rest, depleting our savings (natural capital) for the future.
steps: First, understand carrying capacity: the maximum population size an environment can sustain indefinitely. For humans, this is complex due to technology and trade.
Next, learn about the Ecological Footprint (EF): a model that measures how much land and water area a human population requires to produce the resources it consumes and to absorb its waste.

Then, compare the EF to biocapacity. Biocapacity is the biologically productive area available to provide the resources we use and to absorb our waste. If EF > biocapacity, we have an ecological deficit.
Finally, analyse global trends. As a whole, humanity is in ecological overshoot, meaning our collective EF exceeds Earth's biocapacity. This highlights the need for sustainable resource management.

flashcards.back: The maximum number of individuals of a species that can be sustainably supported by a given environment. For humans, it is highly dynamic due to technology and resource substitution.

A model that measures the area of biologically productive land and water required to provide the resources a person or population consumes and to absorb their waste, under prevailing technology. Measured in global hectares (gha).

The capacity of a given biologically productive area to generate an on-going supply of renewable resources and to absorb its spillover waste. It represents the 'supply' side of the sustainability equation.
The unit of measurement for both Ecological Footprint and biocapacity. It represents a hectare with world-average biological productivity for a given year.

Occurs when a population's demand on an ecosystem (its Ecological Footprint) exceeds the capacity of that ecosystem to regenerate (its biocapacity). Global overshoot means humanity is using more than the Earth can regenerate in a year.
The stock of natural resources and services that yield a flow of future benefits. Examples include forests, fisheries, and mineral deposits.
The yield or harvest obtained from natural capital. Living sustainably means not consuming more than the natural income.
Natural resources that can be replenished over time through natural processes, as long as the rate of consumption does not exceed the rate of regeneration. E.g., timber, solar energy.
Natural resources that exist in finite quantities and are consumed much faster than they are formed. E.g., fossil fuels, minerals.
A nation is in ecological deficit if its footprint exceeds its own biocapacity. It is an ecological creditor if its biocapacity exceeds its footprint.

Avoid stating a single number for human carrying capacity. It's not fixed. It depends on technology, resource distribution, consumption patterns, and waste management. Your answer should reflect this complexity.

flashcards.front: Common Trap: EF vs Carbon Footprint
flashcards.back: The carbon footprint is a component of the Ecological Footprint, not the whole thing. The EF also includes cropland, grazing land, fishing grounds, and built-up land. Be precise in your terminology.

faq.q: Why is calculating carrying capacity for humans so difficult?
faq.a: It's difficult because humans are not like other species. We use technology to change our environment, trade resources globally, and have vastly different consumption patterns. A person in a high-income country might use 10 times the resources of a person in a low-income country. Therefore, human carrying capacity depends on our choices: what we eat, how much energy we use, and what technologies we develop.

faq.q: What is the difference between an Ecological Footprint and a Carbon Footprint?
faq.a: The Carbon Footprint is a component of the Ecological Footprint. The Carbon Footprint measures the total greenhouse gas emissions caused by an individual, event, organisation, or product. In the EF model, this is converted into the area of forest land required to absorb these CO2 emissions. The Ecological Footprint is broader, also including the land needed for food, fibre, and infrastructure, making it a more comprehensive measure of our demand on nature.

faq.q: Is it possible for humanity to reduce its global Ecological Footprint?
faq.a: Yes, it is possible. It requires a combination of individual and collective actions. Strategies include: shifting to renewable energy sources, improving energy efficiency, reducing consumption of meat and animal products, minimising waste through reduction, reuse, and recycling, and investing in technologies and infrastructure that support a low-carbon, sustainable society. It also involves addressing population growth through education and empowerment.

sections.content: As the human population has grown exponentially, so has our demand for Earth's resources. This topic investigates the fundamental question of sustainability: can the Earth support our current and future demands? We will explore the concepts of carrying capacity and the Ecological Footprint, a powerful model for understanding the relationship between human systems and the planet's finite resources.
Human Carrying Capacity: A Complex Calculation

Carrying capacity ($K$) is a concept typically applied to animal populations, representing the maximum population size that an environment can sustain. For humans, this is not a simple calculation. Unlike other species, we have overcome many limiting factors through technology, such as the Haber-Bosch process for creating nitrogen fertilisers, which dramatically increased food production. We can also import resources from across the globe, meaning a local population is not limited by its immediate environment. Therefore, human carrying capacity is not a fixed value but a dynamic concept influenced by cultural, economic, political, and technological factors.

items: Human carrying capacity is difficult to quantify because of our ability to innovate and trade.
Technological advancements can increase carrying capacity (e.g., agricultural revolution).
High levels of consumption in MEDCs can reduce the total number of people the Earth can support sustainably.
The choice of resources used (e.g., renewable vs. non-renewable) significantly impacts carrying capacity.

sections.content: Measuring Our Demand: The Ecological Footprint (EF)

The Ecological Footprint is a model used to quantify our demand on the planet's resources. It translates our consumption into the equivalent land area required to support it. This includes the land to grow our food, produce fibre for clothing, supply timber for construction, and absorb our carbon dioxide emissions from burning fossil fuels. The standard unit is the global hectare (gha), a hectare of land with world-average productivity. By summing up these demands, we can compare our total footprint to the Earth's total available biocapacity.

sections.question:
A country has a population of 20 million people. Its total Ecological Footprint is 90 million global hectares (gha) and its total biocapacity is 30 million gha. 

a) Calculate the per capita Ecological Footprint. 
b) Calculate the per capita biocapacity. 
c) Determine if the country is in an ecological deficit or has an ecological reserve, and by how much per capita.
sections.solution:
a) Per capita Ecological Footprint = Total EF / Population
= 90,000,000 gha / 20,000,000 people
= 4.5 gha per capita [1 mark]

b) Per capita biocapacity = Total biocapacity / Population
= 30,000,000 gha / 20,000,000 people
= 1.5 gha per capita [1 mark]

c) Comparison: The per capita EF (4.5 gha) is greater than the per capita biocapacity (1.5 gha). [1 mark for comparison]

Ecological Deficit = Per capita EF - Per capita biocapacity
= 4.5 gha - 1.5 gha = 3.0 gha per capita deficit. [1 mark]

Conclusion: The country is in an ecological deficit. It is using resources at a rate three times higher than its ecosystems can regenerate.

sections.content: Global and National Trends: Overshoot and Inequity

On a global scale, humanity's Ecological Footprint has exceeded the Earth's biocapacity since the 1970s. This situation is termed 'ecological overshoot'. We are currently using the equivalent of about 1.7 Earths to provide the resources we use and absorb our waste. This is only possible by liquidating our natural capital – for example, by overfishing, depleting freshwater sources, and allowing carbon dioxide to accumulate in the atmosphere. There is also significant inequity. High-income countries (MEDCs) typically have a per capita EF far greater than the global average biocapacity, while many low-income countries (LEDCs) have a footprint below this threshold. This means MEDCs are effectively using the biocapacity of other nations to support their lifestyles.

When evaluating the Ecological Footprint model, remember to discuss both its strengths and weaknesses. Strengths include its power as a communication tool and its ability to quantify sustainability. Weaknesses include the fact that it doesn't account for all environmental impacts (e.g., water pollution, biodiversity loss not related to land use) and the complexity of the data can lead to inaccuracies.

sections.question:
The global average available biocapacity is approximately 1.6 gha per person. The average citizen of the USA has an EF of 8.1 gha, while the average citizen of Nigeria has an EF of 1.2 gha. 

a) Calculate how many 'Earths' would be needed if everyone lived like the average US citizen. 
b) Evaluate the sustainability and equity implications of these figures.
sections.solution:
a) Number of Earths = EF per person / Available biocapacity per person
= 8.1 gha / 1.6 gha
= 5.06 Earths [1 mark]
If the entire world population lived like the average US citizen, we would require more than five planets to support us. [1 mark]

b) Evaluation:
Sustainability: The US lifestyle is highly unsustainable as its EF (8.1 gha) is vastly greater than the available biocapacity (1.6 gha). The Nigerian lifestyle is currently sustainable on a per capita basis as its EF (1.2 gha) is below the available biocapacity. [1 mark]

Equity: The data reveals a massive inequity in resource consumption. The high EF of the US citizen is placing a disproportionate strain on global resources, effectively reducing the 'environmental space' available for others. This raises ethical questions about the right to development and fair resource allocation. [1 mark]

Implication: To achieve global sustainability, high-consuming nations must significantly reduce their footprints, while developing nations may need to increase their resource use to improve human well-being, but do so in a sustainable way ('leapfrogging' to green technologies). [1 mark for a concluding point]
